''Wpt-rnp.t'' |- !colspan="2" |Festival of the Rising of [[:en:Sopdet|Sopdet]] |- | class="hiero" colspan="2" |W3-O1-D21:X1-D54-M44-X1:N14 |- |colspan="2" |Heb-peret-Sopdet
''Ḥb-prt-Spdt'' |- !colspan="2" |Sopdet and Wepet-renpet |- | class="hiero" colspan="2" |R8-M44-N14:X1*H8-Z9*X1*H8:V30-F13-M4 |- |colspan="2" |"Sopdet the Great,
Lady of Wepet-renpet."R. A. Parker: ''The Calendars of Ancient Egypt.'' Chicago (Illinois) 1950, p. 33, § 168. |}
The [[:en:Ancient Egypt|ancient Egyptian]] '''Sothis Festival''' celebrated the Wepet-renpet event, the [[heliacal rising]] of Sothis. This event determined the start of the year in the [[Lunar calendar (Ancient Egypt)|lunar calendar]]. The festival is attested in the [[:en:Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom]] but should not be confused with the [[Egyptian New Year|New Year festival]] in the [[:en:Egyptian civil calendar|administrative calendar]]. == Background == [[File:Sopdet.svg|thumb|upright|The goddess Sopdet]] Since the lunar calendar year always began with the sunrise after the new moon, Wepet-renpet usually occurred a few days before the year's start. This typically fell in the month of Wepet-renpet, the 12th month of the lunar calendar, though occasionally in an intercalary month depending on the theory. According to a hypothesis in {{ill|The Calendars of Ancient Egypt|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Calendars of Ancient Egypt|qq=}}, an intercalary 13th month was inserted into the Sothic year whenever the following month began fewer than 12 days after the Wepet-renpet event.R. A. Parker: ''The Calendars of Ancient Egypt.'' Chicago (Illinois) 1950, p. 31, § 151. This would mean that the Wepet-renpet event always took place in its eponymous month, and the new year could start more than a month after the Sothis Festival. Alternatively, an intercalary month could be added whenever the Wepet-renpet event fell outside its designated month, potentially aligning the Sothis Festival with the new year. According to {{ill|Ancient Egyptian festival dates|de|Altägyptische Festdaten|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Egyptian festival dates|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}}, annual festivals were eventually fixed in the administrative calendar, and the Sothis Festival was combined with the New Year festival "Wepet-renpet," beginning the eponymous first month of the administrative calendar. Over time, it moved away from the rising of Sirius, retrograding through the seasons.S. Schott: ''Altägyptische Festdaten.'' Mainz et al., 1950, p. (46). == Mythology == In the Old Kingdom, Sothis appeared in the [[:en:Pyramid Texts|Pyramid Texts]] (PT) as a form of the goddess [[:en:Isis|Isis]] or as the [[:en:Ba (Egyptian soul)|Ba]] accompanying the [[Sah (constellation)|Sah]] constellation. In PT 883, the deceased and rejuvenated king is crowned by [[:en:Osiris|Osiris]] and Sothis in her role as the "Year." The brightness of Sothis was seen as an omen for a good or bad year ahead. == Festival Proceedings == Due to a lack of direct evidence, the exact proceedings of the Sothis Festival cannot be reconstructed. Only three entries in festival lists from the Middle and New Kingdoms have survived. By the Middle Kingdom, the festival resembled a necropolis celebration, with grave visitors solicited for [[Offering (religion)|offerings]]. A fragment of a [[:en:Manuscript|manuscript]] notes that in the seventh year of [[:en:Sesostris III|Sesostris III]], the Sothis Festival was held on [[Peret (Ancient Egypt)#Dating|17 Peret IV]]. Supplies for the festival included "200 different breads and 60 jars of beer." == Literature == * [[:en:Rolf Krauss|Rolf Krauss]]: ''Sothis- und Monddaten. Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'' (= ''Hildesheimer Ägyptologische Beiträge (HÄB).'' Band 20). Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}}. * {{Literatur|Titel=The Calendars of Ancient Egypt |Autor=Richard Anthony Parker |Sammelwerk=Studies in Ancient Oriental Civilization |Band=26 |Verlag=The University of Chicago Press |Ort=Chicago (Illinois) |Datum=1950 |Sprache=en }} * [[Siegfried Schott]]: ''Altägyptische Festdaten'' (= ''Akademie der Wissenschaften und der Literatur. Abhandlungen der Geistes- und Sozialwissenschaftlichen Klasse (AM-GS).'' 1950, Band 10, {{ISSN|0002-2977}}). Verlag der Akademie der Wissenschaften und der Literatur, Mainz et al., 1950. == References == {{translated page|de|Sothis-Fest|version=231221368|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian festivals]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] }} [[de:Sothis-Fest]] {{Egyptian Calendar Navbar}} [[Category:Festival Calendar of the Month Achet 1]] [[Category:Ancient_Egyptian_festivals]] {{wikidata link|Q2303813}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata no English label]] {{wikidata link|Q2303813}} [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

In PT 883, the deceased and rejuvenated king is crowned by [[:en:Osiris|Osiris]] and Sothis in her role as the "Year." The brightness of Sothis was seen as an omen for a good or bad year ahead. == Festival Proceedings == Due to a lack of direct evidence, the exact proceedings of the Sothis Festival cannot be reconstructed. Only three entries in festival lists from the Middle and New Kingdoms have survived. By the Middle Kingdom, the festival resembled a necropolis celebration, with grave visitors solicited for [[Offering (religion)|offerings]]. A fragment of a [[:en:Manuscript|manuscript]] notes that in the seventh year of [[:en:Sesostris III|Sesostris III]], the Sothis Festival was held on [[Peret (Ancient Egypt)#Dating|17 Peret IV]]. Supplies for the festival included "200 different breads and 60 jars of beer." == Literature == * [[:en:Rolf Krauss|Rolf Krauss]]: ''Sothis- und Monddaten. Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'' (= ''Hildesheimer Ägyptologische Beiträge (HÄB).'' Band 20). Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}}. * {{Literatur|Titel=The Calendars of Ancient Egypt |Autor=Richard Anthony Parker |Sammelwerk=Studies in Ancient Oriental Civilization |Band=26 |Verlag=The University of Chicago Press |Ort=Chicago (Illinois) |Datum=1950 |Sprache=en }} * [[Siegfried Schott]]: ''Altägyptische Festdaten'' (= ''Akademie der Wissenschaften und der Literatur. Abhandlungen der Geistes- und Sozialwissenschaftlichen Klasse (AM-GS).'' 1950, Band 10, {{ISSN|0002-2977}}). Verlag der Akademie der Wissenschaften und der Literatur, Mainz et al., 1950. == References == {{translated page|de|Sothis-Fest|version=231221368|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian festivals]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] }} [[de:Sothis-Fest]] {{Egyptian Calendar Navbar}} [[Category:Festival Calendar of the Month Achet 1]] [[Category:Ancient_Egyptian_festivals]] {{wikidata link|Q2303813}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata no English label]] {{wikidata link|Q2303813}} [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]]

''Ḥb-prt-Spdt'' |- !colspan="2" |Sopdet and Wepet-renpet |- | class="hiero" colspan="2" |R8-M44-N14:X1*H8-Z9*X1*H8:V30-F13-M4 |- |colspan="2" |"Sopdet the Great,
Lady of Wepet-renpet."R. A. Parker: ''The Calendars of Ancient Egypt.'' Chicago (Illinois) 1950, p. 33, § 168. |}
The [[:en:Ancient Egypt|ancient Egyptian]] '''Sothis Festival''' celebrated the Wepet-renpet event, the [[heliacal rising]] of Sothis. This event determined the start of the year in the [[Lunar calendar (Ancient Egypt)|lunar calendar]]. The festival is attested in the [[:en:Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom]] but should not be confused with the [[Egyptian New Year|New Year festival]] in the [[:en:Egyptian civil calendar|administrative calendar]]. == Background == [[File:Sopdet.svg|thumb|upright|The goddess Sopdet]] Since the lunar calendar year always began with the sunrise after the new moon, Wepet-renpet usually occurred a few days before the year's start. This typically fell in the month of Wepet-renpet, the 12th month of the lunar calendar, though occasionally in an intercalary month depending on the theory. According to a hypothesis in {{ill|The Calendars of Ancient Egypt|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Calendars of Ancient Egypt|qq=}}, an intercalary 13th month was inserted into the Sothic year whenever the following month began fewer than 12 days after the Wepet-renpet event.R. A. Parker: ''The Calendars of Ancient Egypt.'' Chicago (Illinois) 1950, p. 31, § 151. This would mean that the Wepet-renpet event always took place in its eponymous month, and the new year could start more than a month after the Sothis Festival. Alternatively, an intercalary month could be added whenever the Wepet-renpet event fell outside its designated month, potentially aligning the Sothis Festival with the new year. According to {{ill|Ancient Egyptian festival dates|de|Altägyptische Festdaten|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Egyptian festival dates|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}}, annual festivals were eventually fixed in the administrative calendar, and the Sothis Festival was combined with the New Year festival "Wepet-renpet," beginning the eponymous first month of the administrative calendar. Over time, it moved away from the rising of Sirius, retrograding through the seasons.S. Schott: ''Altägyptische Festdaten.'' Mainz et al., 1950, p. (46). == Mythology == In the Old Kingdom, Sothis appeared in the [[:en:Pyramid Texts|Pyramid Texts]] (PT) as a form of the goddess [[:en:Isis|Isis]] or as the [[:en:Ba (Egyptian soul)|Ba]] accompanying the [[Sah (constellation)|Sah]] constellation. In PT 883, the deceased and rejuvenated king is crowned by [[:en:Osiris|Osiris]] and Sothis in her role as the "Year." The brightness of Sothis was seen as an omen for a good or bad year ahead. == Festival Proceedings == Due to a lack of direct evidence, the exact proceedings of the Sothis Festival cannot be reconstructed. Only three entries in festival lists from the Middle and New Kingdoms have survived. By the Middle Kingdom, the festival resembled a necropolis celebration, with grave visitors solicited for [[Offering (religion)|offerings]]. A fragment of a [[:en:Manuscript|manuscript]] notes that in the seventh year of [[:en:Sesostris III|Sesostris III]], the Sothis Festival was held on [[Peret (Ancient Egypt)#Dating|17 Peret IV]]. Supplies for the festival included "200 different breads and 60 jars of beer." == Literature == * [[:en:Rolf Krauss|Rolf Krauss]]: ''Sothis- und Monddaten. Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'' (= ''Hildesheimer Ägyptologische Beiträge (HÄB).'' Band 20). Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}}. * {{Literatur|Titel=The Calendars of Ancient Egypt |Autor=Richard Anthony Parker |Sammelwerk=Studies in Ancient Oriental Civilization |Band=26 |Verlag=The University of Chicago Press |Ort=Chicago (Illinois) |Datum=1950 |Sprache=en }} * [[Siegfried Schott]]: ''Altägyptische Festdaten'' (= ''Akademie der Wissenschaften und der Literatur. Abhandlungen der Geistes- und Sozialwissenschaftlichen Klasse (AM-GS).'' 1950, Band 10, {{ISSN|0002-2977}}). Verlag der Akademie der Wissenschaften und der Literatur, Mainz et al., 1950. == References == {{translated page|de|Sothis-Fest|version=231221368|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian festivals]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] }} [[de:Sothis-Fest]] {{Egyptian Calendar Navbar}} [[Category:Festival Calendar of the Month Achet 1]] [[Category:Ancient_Egyptian_festivals]] {{wikidata link|Q2303813}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata no English label]] {{wikidata link|Q2303813}}

A. Parker: ''The Calendars of Ancient Egypt.'' Chicago (Illinois) 1950, p. 31, § 151. This would mean that the Wepet-renpet event always took place in its eponymous month, and the new year could start more than a month after the Sothis Festival. Alternatively, an intercalary month could be added whenever the Wepet-renpet event fell outside its designated month, potentially aligning the Sothis Festival with the new year. According to {{ill|Ancient Egyptian festival dates|de|Altägyptische Festdaten|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Egyptian festival dates|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}}, annual festivals were eventually fixed in the administrative calendar, and the Sothis Festival was combined with the New Year festival "Wepet-renpet," beginning the eponymous first month of the administrative calendar. Over time, it moved away from the rising of Sirius, retrograding through the seasons.S. Schott: ''Altägyptische Festdaten.'' Mainz et al., 1950, p. (46). == Mythology == In the Old Kingdom, Sothis appeared in the [[:en:Pyramid Texts|Pyramid Texts]] (PT) as a form of the goddess [[:en:Isis|Isis]] or as the [[:en:Ba (Egyptian soul)|Ba]] accompanying the [[Sah (constellation)|Sah]] constellation. In PT 883, the deceased and rejuvenated king is crowned by [[:en:Osiris|Osiris]] and Sothis in her role as the "Year." The brightness of Sothis was seen as an omen for a good or bad year ahead. == Festival Proceedings == Due to a lack of direct evidence, the exact proceedings of the Sothis Festival cannot be reconstructed. Only three entries in festival lists from the Middle and New Kingdoms have survived. By the Middle Kingdom, the festival resembled a necropolis celebration, with grave visitors solicited for [[Offering (religion)|offerings]]. A fragment of a [[:en:Manuscript|manuscript]] notes that in the seventh year of [[:en:Sesostris III|Sesostris III]], the Sothis Festival was held on [[Peret (Ancient Egypt)#Dating|17 Peret IV]]. Supplies for the festival included "200 different breads and 60 jars of beer." == Literature == * [[:en:Rolf Krauss|Rolf Krauss]]: ''Sothis- und Monddaten. Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'' (= ''Hildesheimer Ägyptologische Beiträge (HÄB).'' Band 20). Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}}. * {{Literatur|Titel=The Calendars of Ancient Egypt |Autor=Richard Anthony Parker |Sammelwerk=Studies in Ancient Oriental Civilization |Band=26 |Verlag=The University of Chicago Press |Ort=Chicago (Illinois) |Datum=1950 |Sprache=en }} * [[Siegfried Schott]]: ''Altägyptische Festdaten'' (= ''Akademie der Wissenschaften und der Literatur. Abhandlungen der Geistes- und Sozialwissenschaftlichen Klasse (AM-GS).'' 1950, Band 10, {{ISSN|0002-2977}}). Verlag der Akademie der Wissenschaften und der Literatur, Mainz et al., 1950. == References == {{translated page|de|Sothis-Fest|version=231221368|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian festivals]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] }} [[de:Sothis-Fest]] {{Egyptian Calendar Navbar}} [[Category:Festival Calendar of the Month Achet 1]] [[Category:Ancient_Egyptian_festivals]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata no English label]] {{wikidata link|Q2303813}}

The [[:en:Ancient Egypt|ancient Egyptian]] '''Sothis Festival''' celebrated the Wepet-renpet event, the [[heliacal rising]] of Sothis. This event determined the start of the year in the [[Lunar calendar (Ancient Egypt)|lunar calendar]]. The festival is attested in the [[:en:Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom]] but should not be confused with the [[Egyptian New Year|New Year festival]] in the [[:en:Egyptian civil calendar|administrative calendar]]. == Background == [[File:Sopdet.svg|thumb|upright|The goddess Sopdet]] Since the lunar calendar year always began with the sunrise after the new moon, Wepet-renpet usually occurred a few days before the year's start. This typically fell in the month of Wepet-renpet, the 12th month of the lunar calendar, though occasionally in an intercalary month depending on the theory. According to a hypothesis in {{ill|The Calendars of Ancient Egypt|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Calendars of Ancient Egypt|qq=}}, an intercalary 13th month was inserted into the Sothic year whenever the following month began fewer than 12 days after the Wepet-renpet event.R. A. Parker: ''The Calendars of Ancient Egypt.'' Chicago (Illinois) 1950, p. 31, § 151. This would mean that the Wepet-renpet event always took place in its eponymous month, and the new year could start more than a month after the Sothis Festival. Alternatively, an intercalary month could be added whenever the Wepet-renpet event fell outside its designated month, potentially aligning the Sothis Festival with the new year. According to {{ill|Ancient Egyptian festival dates|de|Altägyptische Festdaten|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Egyptian festival dates|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}}, annual festivals were eventually fixed in the administrative calendar, and the Sothis Festival was combined with the New Year festival "Wepet-renpet," beginning the eponymous first month of the administrative calendar. Over time, it moved away from the rising of Sirius, retrograding through the seasons.S. Schott: ''Altägyptische Festdaten.'' Mainz et al., 1950, p. (46). == Mythology == In the Old Kingdom, Sothis appeared in the [[:en:Pyramid Texts|Pyramid Texts]] (PT) as a form of the goddess [[:en:Isis|Isis]] or as the [[:en:Ba (Egyptian soul)|Ba]] accompanying the [[Sah (constellation)|Sah]] constellation. In PT 883, the deceased and rejuvenated king is crowned by [[:en:Osiris|Osiris]] and Sothis in her role as the "Year." The brightness of Sothis was seen as an omen for a good or bad year ahead. == Festival Proceedings == Due to a lack of direct evidence, the exact proceedings of the Sothis Festival cannot be reconstructed. Only three entries in festival lists from the Middle and New Kingdoms have survived. By the Middle Kingdom, the festival resembled a necropolis celebration, with grave visitors solicited for [[Offering (religion)|offerings]]. A fragment of a [[:en:Manuscript|manuscript]] notes that in the seventh year of [[:en:Sesostris III|Sesostris III]], the Sothis Festival was held on [[Peret (Ancient Egypt)#Dating|17 Peret IV]]. Supplies for the festival included "200 different breads and 60 jars of beer." == Literature == * [[:en:Rolf Krauss|Rolf Krauss]]: ''Sothis- und Monddaten. Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'' (= ''Hildesheimer Ägyptologische Beiträge (HÄB).'' Band 20). Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}}. * {{Literatur|Titel=The Calendars of Ancient Egypt |Autor=Richard Anthony Parker |Sammelwerk=Studies in Ancient Oriental Civilization |Band=26 |Verlag=The University of Chicago Press |Ort=Chicago (Illinois) |Datum=1950 |Sprache=en }} * [[Siegfried Schott]]: ''Altägyptische Festdaten'' (= ''Akademie der Wissenschaften und der Literatur. Abhandlungen der Geistes- und Sozialwissenschaftlichen Klasse (AM-GS).'' 1950, Band 10, {{ISSN|0002-2977}}). Verlag der Akademie der Wissenschaften und der Literatur, Mainz et al., 1950. == References == {{translated page|de|Sothis-Fest|version=231221368|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| }} [[de:Sothis-Fest]] {{Egyptian Calendar Navbar}} {{wikidata link|Q2303813}} [[Category:Ancient Egyptian festivals]] [[Category:Ancient_Egyptian_festivals]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Festival Calendar of the Month Achet 1]] [[Category:Wikidata no English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]]

[[de:Sothis-Fest]] [[de:Sothis-Fest]] [[de:Sothis-Fest]]
{| !colspan="2" |{{ill|Wepet-renpet|lt=Wepet-renpet|lt=Opener of the Year|de|Wepet-renpet|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Opener of the Year|qq=|WD=Q2559877}} |- | class="hiero" |F14alsoF15 |[[Wepet-renpet]]
''Wpt-rnp.t'' |- !colspan="2" |Festival of the Rising of {{ill|Sopdet|en|Sopdet|WD=Q930167|lt=Sopdet}} |- | class="hiero" colspan="2" |W3-O1-D21:X1-D54-M44-X1:N14 |- |colspan="2" |Heb-peret-Sopdet
''Ḥb-prt-Spdt'' |- !colspan="2" |Sopdet and Wepet-renpet |- | class="hiero" colspan="2" |R8-M44-N14:X1*H8-Z9*X1*H8:V30-F13-M4 |- |colspan="2" |"Sopdet the Great,
Lady of Wepet-renpet."R. A. Parker: ''The Calendars of Ancient Egypt.'' Chicago (Illinois) 1950, p. 33, § 168. |}
The {{ill|Ancient Egypt|en|Ancient Egypt|WD=Q11768|lt=ancient Egyptian}} '''Sothis Festival''' celebrated the Wepet-renpet event, the [[heliacal rising]] of Sothis. This event determined the start of the year in the [[Lunar calendar (Ancient Egypt)|lunar calendar]]. The festival is attested in the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|en|Old Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q177819|lt=Old Kingdom}} but should not be confused with the [[Egyptian New Year|New Year festival]] in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|en|Egyptian civil calendar|WD=Q108862211|lt=administrative calendar}}. == Background == [[File:Sopdet.svg|thumb|upright|The goddess Sopdet]] Since the lunar calendar year always began with the sunrise after the new moon, Wepet-renpet usually occurred a few days before the year's start. This typically fell in the month of Wepet-renpet, the 12th month of the lunar calendar, though occasionally in an intercalary month depending on the theory. According to a hypothesis in {{ill|The Calendars of Ancient Egypt|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Calendars of Ancient Egypt|qq=}}, an intercalary 13th month was inserted into the Sothic year whenever the following month began fewer than 12 days after the Wepet-renpet event.R. A. Parker: ''The Calendars of Ancient Egypt.'' Chicago (Illinois) 1950, p. 31, § 151. This would mean that the Wepet-renpet event always took place in its eponymous month, and the new year could start more than a month after the Sothis Festival. Alternatively, an intercalary month could be added whenever the Wepet-renpet event fell outside its designated month, potentially aligning the Sothis Festival with the new year. According to {{ill|Ancient Egyptian festival dates|de|Altägyptische Festdaten|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Egyptian festival dates|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}}, annual festivals were eventually fixed in the administrative calendar, and the Sothis Festival was combined with the New Year festival "Wepet-renpet," beginning the eponymous first month of the administrative calendar. Over time, it moved away from the rising of Sirius, retrograding through the seasons.S. Schott: ''Altägyptische Festdaten.'' Mainz et al., 1950, p. (46). == Mythology == In the Old Kingdom, Sothis appeared in the {{ill|Pyramid Texts|en|Pyramid Texts|WD=Q645882|lt=Pyramid Texts}} (PT) as a form of the goddess {{ill|Isis|en|Isis|WD=Q79876|lt=Isis}} or as the {{ill|Ba (Egyptian soul)|en|Ba (Egyptian soul)|WD=Q1129404|lt=Ba}} accompanying the [[Sah (constellation)|Sah]] constellation. In PT 883, the deceased and rejuvenated king is crowned by {{ill|Osiris|en|Osiris|WD=Q46491|lt=Osiris}} and Sothis in her role as the "Year." The brightness of Sothis was seen as an omen for a good or bad year ahead. == Festival Proceedings == Due to a lack of direct evidence, the exact proceedings of the Sothis Festival cannot be reconstructed. Only three entries in festival lists from the Middle and New Kingdoms have survived. By the Middle Kingdom, the festival resembled a necropolis celebration, with grave visitors solicited for [[Offering (religion)|offerings]]. A fragment of a {{ill|Manuscript|en|Manuscript|WD=Q87167|lt=manuscript}} notes that in the seventh year of [[:en:Sesostris III|Sesostris III]], the Sothis Festival was held on [[Peret (Ancient Egypt)#Dating|17 Peret IV]]. Supplies for the festival included "200 different breads and 60 jars of beer." == Literature == * [[:en:Rolf Krauss|Rolf Krauss]]: ''Sothis- und Monddaten. Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'' (= ''Hildesheimer Ägyptologische Beiträge (HÄB).'' Band 20). Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}}. * {{Literatur|Titel=The Calendars of Ancient Egypt |Autor=Richard Anthony Parker |Sammelwerk=Studies in Ancient Oriental Civilization |Band=26 |Verlag=The University of Chicago Press |Ort=Chicago (Illinois) |Datum=1950 |Sprache=en }} * [[Siegfried Schott]]: ''Altägyptische Festdaten'' (= ''Akademie der Wissenschaften und der Literatur. Abhandlungen der Geistes- und Sozialwissenschaftlichen Klasse (AM-GS).'' 1950, Band 10, {{ISSN|0002-2977}}). Verlag der Akademie der Wissenschaften und der Literatur, Mainz et al., 1950. == References == {{translated page|de|Sothis-Fest|version=231221368|comment=Imported full de history}} [[de:Sothis-Fest]] {{Egyptian Calendar Navbar}} {{wikidata link|Q2303813}} [[Category:Ancient Egyptian festivals]] [[Category:Ancient_Egyptian_festivals]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Festival Calendar of the Month Achet 1]] [[Category:Wikidata no English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:pages]]
